content.assessment: This proposal operates directly on fiscal magnitude and capacity allocation: capping or reducing the ~25,962 stagnant state-sector place count against falling cohorts is a structural resource-reallocation lever, distinct from admission-rule redesign. Given Hungary's capitation-based financing (F-live-education_finance-05), reducing selective-track capacity while cohorts shrink could, in principle, redirect per-capita funding and retained high-performing peers back toward comprehensive schools — addressing the underlying fiscal-incidence problem more directly than admissions reform alone. However, the proposal's own risk analysis flags that church/foundation schools outside Klebelsberg jurisdiction could absorb displaced demand, which from a finance perspective would mean state-sector fiscal contraction without a corresponding reduction in overall selective-track resourcing or segregation, simply shifting cost incidence to a different sector with different (and less transparent) funding rules.
opportunities: If capacity contraction is paired with the SES-aware admissions design of TP-live-t1, the combined fiscal and access effects could be more equity-favorable than either alone
strengths: Directly reduces the scale of the capitation-funding leakage from feeder schools by shrinking the destination capacity that drives high-performer exit (F-live-education_finance-05)

Uses demographic decline (falling cohorts) as a lower-conflict resource-reallocation lever rather than requiring new appropriations, which is relevant given Hungary's constrained overall spending envelope (F-live-education_finance-06)
threats: Displacement of demand to church/foundation schools outside state jurisdiction could preserve or worsen aggregate segregation and cost-incidence patterns while appearing to shrink the state-sector footprint on paper (F-live-education_finance-05 is silent on non-state sector capitation rules, limiting ability to assess net fiscal effect)
weaknesses: Does not address the admissions-bias mechanism, so any remaining places may become even more concentrated among socioeconomically advantaged families, worsening cost-incidence concentration even as aggregate capacity falls (F-live-education_finance-02)
No mechanism proposed for compensating feeder schools for capitation losses already incurred under the existing footprint
